─[ Bash Heredoc JSON Injection ]─ ┐

name: bash-heredoc-json-injection description: Heredoc variable interpolation breaks generated JSON/JS files — use node -e + JSON.stringify instead scope: internal user-invocable: false allowed-tools: Bash, Read, Edit, Write

Bash Heredoc JSON Injection

Never use bash heredocs with variable interpolation to generate JSON or JavaScript files. If the variable contains quotes, backslashes, $, or newlines, the output will be malformed or exploitable.

The Problem

# BROKEN — $HUB with quotes/backslashes produces invalid JSON
cat > config.json << EOF
{ "command": "LORE_HUB=\"$HUB\" node \"$HUB/script.js\"" }
EOF

The Fix

Use node -e with process.argv to pass values safely. JSON.stringify handles all escaping.

node -e "
  const fs = require('fs');
  const hub = process.argv[1];
  const config = {
    command: 'LORE_HUB=' + JSON.stringify(hub) + ' node ' + JSON.stringify(hub + '/script.js')
  };
  fs.writeFileSync(process.argv[2], JSON.stringify(config, null, 2) + '\n');
" "$HUB" "$TARGET/config.json"

For generated JS source files, the same pattern applies — build strings with JSON.stringify for safe quoting:

node -e "
  const fs = require('fs');
  const hub = process.argv[1];
  const src = 'process.env.HUB = ' + JSON.stringify(hub) + ';\n';
  fs.writeFileSync(process.argv[2], src);
" "$HUB" "$TARGET/plugin.js"

Snags

  • Heredocs without a quoted delimiter (<< 'EOF') expand variables — this is the root cause
  • Even << 'EOF' (no expansion) doesn’t help when you need the variable value in the output
  • The node -e + process.argv pattern avoids shell interpolation entirely
  • This project already uses this pattern for .lore marker files — apply it consistently to all generated configs
PgUp/PgDn: scroll b: bulk commands q: quit x: menu ← → ↑ ↓: navigate
Donate 0.24.4
